section.title: Written Assignments
section.p: Please see the for the homework due dates.
As the term progresses, more written assignments will be added to the list here. These should be turned in no later than their posted due dates. They can be turn in in-person, or uploaded in D2L.

Written assignments are collectively worth 18% of your final grade. You cannot expect to do well in this course unless you are turning these assignments in. These are where you will get to develop your mathematical writing skills and work on your ability to give a convincing logical argument.

The most important thing to understand about your written homework is that you are not merely finding and giving answers. You are explaining to someone what the problem was, how you handled it, and summarizing the results. In general, your write-ups should respect these four C's:
self-Containment
Your write-up should clearly and explicitly tell the reader what you are trying to accomplish. The most straightforward way to do this is to simply copy the entire exercise statement as the first thing in your write-up.
Clarity
Am I able to read your work? Is your writing legible? Have you written complete sentences that make sense, where appropriate? If you have charts, graphs, and mathematical expressions, have you clearly indicated what they represent? Generally, any graphs need their axes labeled with regularly spaced tick marks, and they need some kind of overall title. If something about your write-up causes me to pause and wonder what you mean, you will lose clarity points.
Convincing
Even if your writing is clear, it might not be logically convincing. For example, I rode my bike to school today, so I only had $5 in my wallet. is clear in the sense that the sentence structure is fine. But I don't understand why riding your bike to school implies that you only have $5 in your wallet. The logical progression of your write-up should convince your reader that you know what you are talking about, and that each statement you write is correct based on other things you have already established in your write-up.
Conclusion
At the end of any write-up, it should be crystal clear what is your actual answer to the original question. In some cases, this will mean that you should write an actual conclusion sentence.
Each exercise will be scored out of 4 points, one for each of these categories. A zero in some category doesn't really mean 0%, it just means I felt there was significant room for improvement and you coudl try again.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 1
paragraphs.p: This assignment has six exercises from the textbook.

Section 3.9 #4. The table shows the population of Nepal (in millions) as of June 30 of the given year. Use a linear approximation to estimate the population at midyear in 1989. Use another linear approximation to predict the population in 2010.

Notes. Don't miss that there are two parts to this exercise. For each part, your write-up should include a formula for a linear function that approximates N(t), and then use that formula at an appropriate value for t. You should properly interpret the result.

Section 3.9 #10. Find the linear approximation of the function g(x)=\sqrt[3]{1+x} at a=0 and use it to approximate the numbers \sqrt[3]{0.95} and \sqrt[3]{1.1}. Illustrate by graphing g and the tangent line.

Notes. For the first two parts, it is up to you to determine the appropriate value for x. For the illustration, provide either an accurate hand-drawn copy of computer-generated graphs, or insert an actual computer-generated image into your write-up.

Section 4.7 #1. The figure shos the graph of a function f. Suppose that Newton's method is used to approximate the root r of the equation f(x)=0 with initial approximation x_1=1.
Draw the thangent lines that are used to find x_2 and x_3, and extimate the numerical values of x_2 and x_3.
Would x_1=5 be a better first approximation? Explain.
[You will have to find the figure in the textbook.]

Notes. You should hand-draw the figure in your write-up. If possible, trace it from the book or a screen (but don't damage your screen). Then use a straight-edge as you hand-draw the relevant lines. In part (b), give a convincing explanation as to why or why not x_1=5 would make a better first approxiation. This may require you to make an additional graph.
Section 4.7 #27. Explain why Newton's method fails when applied to the equation \sqrt[3]{x}=0 with any intial approximation x_1\neq0. Illustrate your explanation with a sketch.
Notes. Give a convincing explanation.

li.p: Show that every member of the family of functions y=(\ln x+C)/x is a solution to the differential equation x^2y'+xy=1.
Illustrate part (a) by graphing several members of the family of solutions on a common screen.
Find a solution of the differential equation that satisfies the initial condition y(1)=2.
Find a solution of the differential equation that satisfies the initial condition y(2)=1.

paragraphs.p: Notes. Part (a): this should be a clear presentation that a generic member of that family would work when substituted into that differential equation. Part (b): provide either an accurate hand-drawn copy of computer-generated graphs, or insert an actual computer-generated image into your write-up. Parts (c) and (d): inlcude whatever algebra steps you take to find the particular solutions these parts ask for.
Section 7.1 #11. Explain why the functions with the given graphs can't be solutions of the differential equation \frac{dy}{dt}=e^t(y-1)^2 [You will have to find the two figures in the textbook.]

Notes. For each graph, there are at least two reasons that are reasonable to show. Consider when various quantities would be negative, zero, or positive in both the context of the equation and of the graph. You could also consider concavity.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 2
paragraphs.p: This assignment has four exercises from the textbook.

Section 7.2 #18. Make a rough sketch of a direction field for the autonomous differential equation y'=f(y), where the graph of f is as shown. How does the limiting behavior of solutions depend on the value of y(0)?
[You will have to find the graph image in the textbook.]

Notes. The graph here is not a graph of y(t). What you are doing here is working with the differential equation y'=f(y), but you are not given a formula for the right side of that differential equation. Instead, you have this graph. For example, the graph shows that when y=0, then f(y) is some relatively large positive number. So in turn, y' would be a relatively large positive number. So on a graph of y(t), if y=0, then the slope of y(t) at that location would be relatively large and positive. This has described how to draw slope field hairs all along the t-axis, which is where y=0. For the last part of the exercise, if you have a graph of y(t), there are five regions on the y-axis for what the value of y(0) could be. You are being asked to describe the long-term consequences for y(t) as t\to\infty, depending on which region y(0) is in.

Section 7.2 #20. A direction field for a differential equation is shown. Draw, with a ruler, the graphs of the Euler approximations to the solution currve that passes through the origin. Use step sizes h=1 and h=0.5. Will the Euler estimates be under-estimates or over-estimates? Explain.
[You will have to find the graph image in the textbook.]

Notes. It is important to accurately present this graph in your response. So I recommend that you take a picture of the image, print it, and then draw on top of that. Also at the end, be sure you are explaining why you feel the estimates are under-estimates or over-estimates.

Section 7.3 #43. A glucose solution is administered intrevenously into the bloodstream at a constant rate r. As the glucose is added, it is converted into other substances and removed from the bloodstream at a rate that is proportional to the concentration at that time. Thus a model for the concentration C=C(t) of the glucose solution in the bloodstream is \frac{dC}{dt}=r-kC where k is a positive constant.
Suppose that the concentration at time t=0 is C_0. Determine the concentration at any time t by solving the differential equation.
Assuming that C_0\lt r/k, find \lim_{t\to\infty}C(t) and interpret your answer.

Notes. Keep in mind that r and k are constants here. C changes over time. You can solve this separable differential equation in the usual way with some algebra rearrangement, then integrating, and then isolating C.

Section 7.3 #49. When a raindrop falls, it increases in size and so its mass at time t is a function of t, namely m(t). The rate of growth of the mass is km(t) for some positive constant k. When we apply Newton's Law of Motion to the raindrop, we get (mv)'=gm, where v is the velocity of the raindrop (directed downward) and g is the acceleration due to gravity. The terminal velocity of the raindrop is \lim_{t\to\infty}v(t). Find an expression for the terminal velocity in terms of g and k.

Notes. This is actually a multipart exercise. Several things could be done in order to reach the final goal of having that expression for the terminal velocity. The following is one outline for solving this.
The rate of growth of the mass is km(t) for some positive constant k is already a small differential equation exercise. You can solve the differential equation here to find an explicit formula for m(t).
Physics (that you don't need to understand if you haven't heard of it before) is saying that next, (mv)'=gm. In that equation, g is a positive constant (roughly 9.8 ), but both m and v change over time. Well, you already found an explicit formula for m(t), so substitute that in. Then execute the prime from the differential equation, applyng the product rule and chain rule as needed. After simplifying, this should leave you with a pretty small differential equation for v with respect to t.
You can solve this differential equation and have an explicit formula for v(t). You may need to remember that in this context, g is a positive number.
Now that you have an explicit formula for v(t), you can take its limit as t\to\infty.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 3
paragraphs.p: This assignment has six exercises from the textbook.

Section 7.5 #1. Suppose that a population develops according to the logistic equation \frac{dP}{dt}=0.05P-0.0005P^2 where t is measured in weeks.
What is the carrying capacity? What is the value of k?
A direction field for this equation is shown. Where are the slopes close to 0? Where are they largest? Which solutions are increasing? Which solutions are decreasing?
[You will have to find the graph image in the textbook.]
Use the direction field to sketch solutions for initial populations of 20, 40, 60, 80, 120, and 140. What do these solutions have in common? How do they differ? Which solutions have inflection points? At what population levels do they occur?
What are the equilibrium solutions? How are the other solutions related to these solutions?
Notes. This differential equation is not presented in the canonical form for a logistic equation. But you can use factoring to convert it into that form.
In part (c), the last question is asking at what population levels do the inflection points occur.

li.p: Show that if P satisfies the logistic equation, then \frac{d^2P}{dt^2}=k^2P\left(1-\frac{P}{M}\right)\left(1-\frac{2P}{M}\right)
Deduce that the population grows fastes when it reaches half its carrying capacity.

paragraphs.p: Notes. Start with the logistic differential euqation and just see what happens when you apply \frac{d}{dt} to each side. You will have to use the product rule and the chain rule (since you will be differentiating with respect to t, but the right side only has P in it). You should keep an eye out for common factors that you can factor out, and not multiply things out too much.
For the second part, recall that a function reaches a maximal slope where the second derivative is zero and the second derivative is transitioning from positive to negative.
Section 8.1 #19. Determine whether the sequence a_n=\frac{(-1)^{n-1}n}{n^2+1} converges or diverges. If it converges, find the limit.

Notes. The function f(x)=\frac{(-1)^{x-1}x}{x^2+1} does not make sense for most real numbers x, because you can't raise -1 to arbitrary powers like \frac12. So you cannot use certain tools like l'Hospital's Rule to analyze this sequence. You will have to use other tools from the section.
Section 8.1 #29. Determine whether the sequence \left\{\frac{(2n-1)!}{(2n+1)!}\right\} converges or diverges. If it converges, find the limit.
Notes. This sequence is only defined for non-negative integers n. So you cannot use certain tools like l'Hospital's Rule to analyze this sequence. You will have to use other tools from the section.

Section 8.1 #40. Use a graph of the sequence to decide whether the sequence is convergent or divergent. If the sequence is convergent, guess the value of the limit from the graph and then prove your guess. a_n=\frac{1\cdot3\cdot5\cdot\cdots\cdot(2n-1)}{(2n)^n}
Notes. You should include a plot. It could be hand-plotted or computer-generated. To prove your guess, see Example 9 from the textbook. This exercise is similar.

Section 8.1 #44. Find the first 40 terms of the sequence defined by a_{n+1}=\begin{cases}\frac12a_n&\text{if }a_n\text{ is an even number}\\3a_n+1&\text{if }a_n\text{ is an odd number}\end{cases} and a_1=11. Do the same if a_1=25. Make a conjecture about this type of sequence.

Notes. Think slowly and carefully when you start working out the numbers in this sequence. For getting started, a_1=11, which is an odd number. So to find a_2, you use the 3a_n+1 formula, and you find a_2=34.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 4
paragraphs.p: This assignment has six exercises from the textbook.

Notes. You may use a computer graphing tool if you would like to, for making the plot. If you make it by hand, make a precise plot. For the second part of the question, you are expected to either prove that the series converges to some specific number or prove that it is divergent. The plot is a helpful start, but proves nothing. You will only earn the point for convincing if you make a convincing argument here.

Notes. Find the helpful way to rewrite \frac{3}{n(n+3)} as \frac{\text{something}}{n}+\frac{\text{something else}}{n+3}. After that, if you need help seeing what happens next, write out the first five or six partial sums. Write them out without adding up the fractions. Look for cancellation instead.

Section 8.2 #44. We have seen that the harmonic series is a divergent series whose terms approach 0. Show that
\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\ln\left(1+\frac{1}{n}\right)
is another series with this property.

Notes. There is a logarithm rule that says \ln(a)+\ln(b)=\ln(ab). Also, it may help to see that 1+\frac{1}{n}=\frac{n+1}{n}. What happens when you have the nth partial sum of this series, and you apply these observations? Can you show that the nth partial sum will grow without bound?
Don't forget to also show that the terms of this series approach 0.

Section 8.2 #52 parts (a) and (b). A certain ball has the property that each time it falls from a height h onto a hard, level surface, it rebounds to a height rh where 0\lt r\lt 1. Suppose that the ball is dropped from an initial height of H meters.
Assuming that the ball continues to bounce indefinitely, find the total distance that it travels.
Calculate the total time that the ball travels. (Use the fact that the ball falls \frac12gt^2 meters in t seconds. On earth, g is about 9.8\,\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}^2}.)

Notes.
On its first drop, it falls H meters. After that, the ball goes up and comes down, repeatedly. So each bounce adds a certain amount of distance traveled. Write this all down as a series, and it should be a geometric series that you can work with.
You know how much distance the ball falls on the second half of its bounce. So you can solve for t to know how much time passes during the second half of its bounce. And you can double that for the full time of its bounce. You can write all this down to give a total amount of time the ball is bouncing, and it should be a geometric series you can work with. Of course, the initial drop is a bit different, so don't include that in your sum.

Section 8.2 #56 Graph the curves y=x^n, 0\leq x\leq1, for n=0,1,2,3,4,\ldots on a common set of axes. By finding the areas between successive curves, give a geometric demonstration of the fact, shown in Example 6, that \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n(n+1)}=1

Notes. The graphs should only be from x=0 to x=1. To find the area between successive curves, use your integral skills. The result for this should be an expression that depends on n, that you can simplify to a single rational expression. Make sure that you explain to the reader what the purpose of this is: that you are showing a visual reason why \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n(n+1)}=1.

Section 8.2 #59 What is wrong with the following calculation?
0\amp=0+0+0+\cdots \amp=(1-1)+(1-1)+(1-1)+\cdots \amp=1-1+1-1+1-1+\cdots \amp=1+(-1+1)+(-1+1)+(-1+1)+\cdots \amp=1+0+0+0+\cdots=1

Notes. Of course the final result that would say 0=1 is wrong. What exaclty happens along the way with these steps, that makes the argument wrong? You should think about each line with \cdots as an infinite series, and from that perspective it is more clear what goes wrong.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 5
paragraphs.p: This assignment has four exercises from the textbook.
Section 8.3 #1. Draw a picture to show that \sum_{n=2}^{\infty}\frac{1}{n^{1.3}}\lt\int_1^{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{1.3}}\,dx What can you conclude about the series?

Notes. You should make a good effort to make the picture accurate, within the limitations of human drawing skills. Since you will want the x-axis to go pretty far (like to at least x=5 if not further) but the y-axis only needs to go up to a little past 1, it will help if you use different scales on your axes.
Section 8.3 #14. Determine whether the series is convergent or divergent. 1+\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{3\sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{4\sqrt{4}}+\frac{1}{5\sqrt{5}}+\cdots
Notes. Ideally you are supposed to figure out the following for yourself, but this is a series where you can pply the Integral Test...

li.p: Use a graph of y=1/x to show that if s_n is the nth partial sum of the harmonic series, then s_n\leq1+\ln(n)
The harmonic series diverges, but very slowly. Use part (a) to show that the sum of the first million terms is less than 15 and the sum of the first billion terms is less than 22.

paragraphs.p: Notes. The purpose of this exercise is to look into how slowly the harmonic series diverges. Ask yourself at the end of this if you appreciate how even after adding a billion terms from this series together, how that partial sum being less than 22 reveals how slowly the partial sums approach infinity.
Section 8.3 #44. Find all positive values of b for which the series \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}b^{\ln n} converges.

Notes. This series is b^{\ln(1)}+b^{\ln(2)}+b^{\ln(3)}+\cdots. First of all, you can use the Test for Divergence to rule out some values of b. Once you have done that, you should be able to use the Integral Test. There is a way that you can rewrite b^{\ln(n)} that will help you do the integral.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 6
paragraphs.p: This assignment has seven exercises from the textbook.

Notes. Your write-up should convince your reader that you are correctly appling the Comparison Test. You should acknowledge that the conditions have been met to even try to use the Comparison Test. When you comparre this series with another series, you should make sure your reader understands why that other series converges or diverges. It could be as simple as citing some small facto about it, or it could be a more involved explanation.
Section 8.3 #29. Determine whether the series is convergent or divergent. \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\sin\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)

Notes. Ideally you are supposed to explore this series and determine which tools are appropriate and helpful. But this series can be successfully studied with either the Limit Comparison Test (my recommendation) or with the Comparison Test.

li.p: Use the sum of the first 10 terms to estimate the sum of the series \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}1/n^2. How good is this estimate?
Improve the estimate using (4) with n=10.
Find a value of n that will ensure that the error in the approximation s\approx s_n is less than 0.001m>.

paragraphs.p: Notes. Part (a) is asking you to use the boxed inequality labeled 3 in the section to understand how far off s_{10} is from the full sum s. Part (b) is asking you to use the boxed inequality labeled 4 in the section, so that you are bounding the full sum in between an under-estimate and an over-estimate.
For part (c), note that you want R_n\lt0.001. You know that R_n\lt\int_n^{\infty}f(x)\,dx, so if you can make it so that \int_n^{\infty}f(x)\,dx\ln0.001, mission acomplished.

Notes. Ideally you are supposed to explore this series and determine which tools are appropriate and helpful. But this series can be successfully studied with the Alternating Series Test. You must persuade your reader that the series meets the conditions to use the Alternating Series Test in the first place.

Section 8.4 #12. Calculate the first 10 partial sums of the series \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n^3} and graph both the sequence of terms and the sequence of partial sums on the same screen. Estimate the error in using the 10th partial sum to approximate the total sum.
Notes. The last part of this exercise is asking you to use the Alternating Series Estimation Theorem from the section.

Notes. You could try to use the Ratio Test, but the limit from that attempt does not exist. Instead, you should directly explore \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left\lvert a_n\right\rvert and see what you find. You may need to use an earlier tool to establish whether or not \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left\lvert a_n\right\rvert converges.

Notes. This is an excellent series to use the Ratio Test with. You will have to carefully simplify some expressions that have factorials in them. If simplified correctly, there will be no more factorials

paragraphs.title: Written HW 7
paragraphs.p: This assignment has six exercises from the textbook.

Section 8.5 #28. Graph the first several partial sums s_n(x) of the series \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}x^n, together with the sum function f(x)=1/(1-x), on a common screen. On what interval do these partial sums appear to be converging to f(x)?
Notes. Your response needs to include computer-generated graphs. You could copy-paste them into a digital document, or print them out separately.
Section 8.5 #33. Suppose the series \sum c_nx^n has radius of convergence 2 and the series \sum d_nx^n has radius of convergence 3. What is the radius of convergence of the series \sum(c_n+d_n)x^n?

Notes. Do all the series here have the same center? First imagine some x-value that is within 2 of that center. Would \sum(c_n+d_n)x^n converge? What about if x is more than 3 away from that center? What if it is in between 2 and 3 units away?
Section 8.5 #35. Is it possible to find a power series whose interval of convergence is [0,\infty)? Explain.

Notes. You must either provide an example of a power series where you can convince your reader that its interval of convergence is [0,\infty), or you must write a convincing argument to your reader that this is impossible.
Section 8.6 #2. Suppose you know that the series \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}b_nx^n converges for |x|\lt2. What can you say about the following series? Why? \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{b_n}{n+1}x^{n+1}
Notes. Think about how the first series can be used to obtain the second.
Section 8.6 #20. Find a power series representation for f(x)=\ln\left(x^2+4\right), and graph f and several partial sums s_n(x) on the same screen. What happens as n increases?
Notes. Your response needs to include computer-generated graphs. You could copy-paste them into a digital document, or print them out separately.

li.p: Show that the function f(x)=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^n}{n!} is a solution of the differential equation f'(x)=f(x).

paragraphs.p: Notes. This is an exercise from section 8.6, before we know about Maclaurin/Taylor series. You are not permitted to simply cite the Maclaurin series for e^x to answer this. Instead, use the series, take its derivative, and make it clear to the reader that the derivative and the original series are the same thing. For part (b), remember from Chapter 7 how to solve the differetial equation y'=y. It is a separable equation. Also you know the initial value of the given series is 1, because f(0)=\left.\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^n}{n!}\right|_{x=0}=\left.1+x+\cdots\right|_{x=0}=1.

paragraphs.title: Written HW 8
paragraphs.p: This assignment has four exercises from the textbook.

Section 8.7 #2. The graph of f is shown. [You will have to find the figure in the textbook.]
Explain why the series
1.6-0.8(x-1)+0.4(x-1)^2-0.1(x-1)^3+\cdots
is not the Taylor series of f centered at 1.
Explain why the series
2.8+0.5(x-2)+1.5(x-2)^2-0.1(x-2)^3+\cdots
is not the Taylor series of f centered at 2.
Notes. To have a write-up that makes sense to a reader, you must include the given graph.
Section 8.7 #40. Use the Maclaurin series for sin(x) to complute \sin 3^\circ correct to five decimal places.
Notes. In radians, 3^\circ is 3\cdot\frac{\pi}{180}=\frac{\pi}{60}.

Notes. Of course your write-up should include the computer-generated graph. The exercise does not ask for this, but it would be nice for you to comment about for which x would f(x)\approx T_3(x) be a good approximation.

Section 8.8 #24. Use the Alternating Series Estimation Theorem or Taylor's Inequality to estimate the range of values of x for which \cos(x)\approx1-\frac{x^2}{2}+\frac{x^4}{24} is accurate to within 0.005.

Notes. While the polynomial here may look like T_4, it is better if you think of it as T_5, with a 0x^5 tacked on to the end. Then if you use Taylor's Inequality, you will get the same error bound as if you used the Alternating Series Estimation Theorem.
